commands.name: /opsx:propose
commands.purpose: Create a change and generate planning artifacts in one step

args.name: change-name-or-description
args.description: Kebab-case name or plain-language change description

commands.description: Creates openspec/changes/<change-name>/；Generates artifacts needed before implementation (for spec-driven: proposal, specs, design, tasks)；Stops when the change is ready for /opsx:apply
commands.example:
You: /opsx:propose add-dark-mode

AI:  Created openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/
     ✓ proposal.md
     ✓ specs/ui/spec.md
     ✓ design.md
     ✓ tasks.md
     Ready for implementation. Run /opsx:apply.
tips: Use this for the fastest end-to-end path
If you want step-by-step artifact control, enable expanded workflows and use /opsx:new + /opsx:continue

commands.name: /opsx:explore
commands.purpose: Think through ideas before committing to a change

args.name: topic
args.description: What you want to explore or investigate

commands.description: Opens an exploratory conversation with no structure required；Investigates the codebase to answer questions；Compares options and approaches；Creates visual diagrams to clarify thinking；Can transition to /opsx:propose (default) or /opsx:new (expanded workflow) when insights crystallize
commands.example:
You: /opsx:explore

AI:  What would you like to explore?

You: How should we handle authentication for the mobile app?

AI:  Let me investigate your current auth setup...
     [Analyzes codebase]

     I see you're using session-based auth for the web app.
     For mobile, we have three main options:

     1. JWT tokens (stateless, works offline)
     2. OAuth2 (if you need third-party login)
     3. Extend current sessions (simpler, but requires connectivity)

     Your API already has CORS configured. Which direction interests you?

You: Let's go with JWT. Can we start a change for that?

AI:  Ready when you are. Run /opsx:propose add-jwt-auth to begin.
tips: Use when requirements are unclear or you need to investigate
No artifacts are created during exploration
Good for comparing multiple approaches before deciding
Can read files and search the codebase

commands.name: /opsx:new

args.name: change-name
args.description: Name for the change folder (prompted if not provided)

args.name: --schema
args.description: Workflow schema to use (default: from config or spec-driven)

commands.description: Creates openspec/changes/<change-name>/ directory；Creates .openspec.yaml metadata file in the change folder；Shows the first artifact template ready for creation；Prompts for change name and schema if not provided
commands.example:
You: /opsx:new add-dark-mode

AI:  Created openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/
     Schema: spec-driven

     Ready to create: proposal
     Use /opsx:continue to create it, or /opsx:ff to create all artifacts.
tips: Use descriptive names: add-feature, fix-bug, refactor-module
Avoid generic names like update, changes, wip
Schema can also be set in project config (openspec/config.yaml)

commands.name: /opsx:continue
commands.purpose: Create the next artifact based on dependencies

args.name: change-name
args.description: Which change to continue (inferred from context if not provided)

commands.description: Queries the artifact dependency graph；Shows which artifacts are ready vs blocked；Creates the first ready artifact；Reads dependency files for context；Shows what becomes available after creation
commands.example:
You: /opsx:continue

AI:  Change: add-dark-mode

     Artifact status:
     ✓ proposal    (done)
     ◆ specs       (ready)
     ◆ design      (ready)
     ○ tasks       (blocked - needs: specs)

     Creating specs...

     [Reads proposal.md, creates specs based on requirements]

     ✓ Created openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/specs/ui/spec.md

     Now available: tasks
     Run /opsx:continue to create the next artifact.
tips: Use when you want to review each artifact before proceeding
Good for complex changes where you want control
Multiple artifacts may become ready simultaneously
You can edit created artifacts before continuing

commands.name: /opsx:ff
commands.purpose: Fast-forward: create all planning artifacts at once

args.name: change-name
args.description: Which change to fast-forward (inferred from context if not provided)

commands.description: Creates all artifacts in dependency order；Tracks progress via todo list；Stops when all apply-required artifacts are complete；Reads each dependency before creating the next artifact
commands.example:
You: /opsx:ff add-dark-mode

AI:  Fast-forwarding add-dark-mode...

     ✓ Creating proposal.md
     ✓ Creating specs/ui/spec.md
     ✓ Creating design.md
     ✓ Creating tasks.md

     All planning artifacts complete!
     Ready for implementation. Run /opsx:apply to begin.
tips: Use when you have a clear picture of what you're building
Faster than /opsx:continue for straightforward changes
You can still edit artifacts afterward
Good for small to medium features

commands.name: /opsx:apply

args.name: change-name
args.description: Which change to implement (inferred from context if not provided)

commands.description: Reads tasks.md and identifies incomplete tasks；Works through tasks one by one；Writes code, creates files, runs tests as needed；Marks tasks complete with checkboxes [x]
commands.example:
You: /opsx:apply

AI:  Implementing add-dark-mode...

     Reading tasks.md:
     - [ ] 1.1 Create ThemeContext
     - [ ] 1.2 Add CSS custom properties
     - [ ] 1.3 Implement localStorage persistence

     Working on 1.1: Create ThemeContext...
     [Creates src/context/ThemeContext.tsx]

     ✓ 1.1 Complete

     Working on 1.2: Add CSS custom properties...
     [Updates src/styles/globals.css]

     ✓ 1.2 Complete
     ...
tips: Can resume where you left off if interrupted
Use for parallel changes by specifying the change name
Completion state is tracked in tasks.md checkboxes

commands.name: /opsx:verify

args.name: change-name
args.description: Which change to verify (inferred from context if not provided)

commands.description: Checks three dimensions of implementation quality；Searches codebase for implementation evidence；Reports issues categorized as CRITICAL, WARNING, or SUGGESTION；Does not block archive, but surfaces issues
commands.example:
You: /opsx:verify

AI:  Verifying add-dark-mode...

     COMPLETENESS
     ✓ All 8 tasks in tasks.md are checked
     ✓ All requirements in specs have corresponding code
     ⚠ Scenario "System preference detection" has no test coverage

     CORRECTNESS
     ✓ Implementation matches spec intent
     ✓ Edge cases from scenarios are handled
     ✓ Error states match spec definitions

     COHERENCE
     ✓ Design decisions reflected in code structure
     ✓ Naming conventions consistent with design.md
     ⚠ Design mentions "CSS variables" but implementation uses Tailwind classes

     SUMMARY
     ─────────────────────────────
     Critical issues: 0
     Warnings: 2
     Ready to archive: Yes (with warnings)

     Recommendations:
     1. Add test for system preference detection
     2. Update design.md to reflect Tailwind usage, or refactor to use CSS variables
tips: Run before archiving to catch mismatches early
Warnings don't block archive but indicate potential issues
Good for reviewing AI's work before committing
Can reveal drift between artifacts and implementation

commands.name: /opsx:sync
commands.purpose: Merge delta specs into main specs

args.name: change-name
args.description: Which change to sync (inferred from context if not provided)

commands.description: Reads delta specs from change folder；Parses ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED/RENAMED sections；Merges changes into main openspec/specs/ directory；Preserves existing content not mentioned in delta；Does not archive the change (remains active)
commands.example:
You: /opsx:sync

AI:  Syncing add-dark-mode delta specs...

     Reading: openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/specs/ui/spec.md
     Target:  openspec/specs/ui/spec.md

     Changes to apply:
     ✓ ADDED: Theme Selection requirement (2 scenarios)
     ✓ ADDED: Accessibility requirement (1 scenario)

     Merging...
     ✓ openspec/specs/ui/spec.md updated

     Change remains active. Run /opsx:archive when ready to complete.
tips: Sync is intelligent, not copy-paste
Can add scenarios to existing requirements without duplicating
Change stays active after sync (not archived)
Most users will never need to call this directly—archive prompts if needed

commands.name: /opsx:archive

args.name: change-name
args.description: Which change to archive (inferred from context if not provided)

commands.description: Checks artifact completion status；Checks task completion (warns if incomplete)；Offers to sync delta specs if not already synced；Moves change folder to openspec/changes/archive/YYYY-MM-DD-<name>/；Preserves all artifacts for audit trail
commands.example:
You: /opsx:archive

AI:  Archiving add-dark-mode...

     Artifact status:
     ✓ proposal.md exists
     ✓ specs/ exists
     ✓ design.md exists
     ✓ tasks.md exists (8/8 tasks complete)

     Delta specs: Not yet synced
     → Sync now? (recommended)

You: Yes

AI:  ✓ Synced specs to openspec/specs/ui/spec.md
     ✓ Moved to openspec/changes/archive/2025-01-24-add-dark-mode/

     Change archived successfully.
tips: Archive won't block on incomplete tasks, but will warn
Delta specs can be synced during archive or beforehand
Archived changes are preserved for history
Use /opsx:verify first to catch issues

commands.name: /opsx:bulk-archive

args.name: change-names
args.description: Specific changes to archive (prompts to select if not provided)

commands.description: Lists all completed changes；Validates each change before archiving；Detects spec conflicts across changes；Resolves conflicts by checking what's actually implemented；Archives in chronological order
commands.example:
You: /opsx:bulk-archive

AI:  Found 3 completed changes:
     - add-dark-mode (8/8 tasks complete)
     - fix-login-bug (3/3 tasks complete)
     - update-footer (2/2 tasks complete)

     Checking for spec conflicts...
     ⚠ add-dark-mode and update-footer both touch specs/ui/

     Inspecting codebase to resolve...
     Both changes are implemented. Will merge in chronological order:
     1. add-dark-mode (created Jan 20)
     2. update-footer (created Jan 22)

     Archive all 3 changes?

You: Yes

AI:  ✓ Archived add-dark-mode
     ✓ Archived fix-login-bug
     ✓ Archived update-footer
     Specs merged: add-dark-mode → update-footer
tips: Good for parallel work streams
Conflict resolution is agentic (checks codebase)
Changes are archived in order of creation
Prompts before overwriting spec content

commands.name: /opsx:onboard
commands.purpose: Guided tutorial through the complete workflow
commands.description: Walks through a complete workflow cycle with narration；Scans your codebase for real improvement opportunities；Creates an actual change with real artifacts；Implements actual work (small, safe changes)；Archives the completed change；Explains each step as it happens
commands.example:
You: /opsx:onboard

AI:  Welcome to OpenSpec!

     I'll walk you through the complete workflow using your actual codebase.
     We'll find something small to improve, create a proper change for it,
     implement it, and archive it.

     Let me scan your codebase for opportunities...

     [Analyzes codebase]

     I found a few things we could work on:
     1. Add input validation to the contact form
     2. Improve error messages in the auth flow
     3. Add loading states to async buttons

     Which interests you? (or suggest something else)
tips: Best for new users learning the workflow
Uses real code, not toy examples
Creates a real change you can keep or discard
Takes 15-30 minutes to complete

legacyCommands.purpose: Create all artifacts at once (proposal, specs, design, tasks)

solutions: Specify the change name explicitly: /opsx:apply add-dark-mode
Check that the change folder exists: openspec list
Verify you're in the right project directory
Run openspec status --change <name> to see what's blocking
Create the schema if it's custom: openspec schema init <name>
Ensure OpenSpec is initialized: openspec init
Check that .claude/skills/ directory exists (for Claude Code)
Restart your AI tool to pick up new skills
Add per-artifact rules for specific guidance
Provide more detail in your change description
Use /opsx:continue instead of /opsx:ff for more control

tools.name: Trae (trae)
tools.commandsLocation: Not generated (no command adapter; use skill-based /openspec-* invocations)

toolSyntax.syntax: Skill-based invocations such as /openspec-propose, /openspec-apply-change (no generated opsx-* command files)

philosophy: Actions, not phases - Commands are things you can do, not stages you're stuck in
Dependencies are enablers - They show what's possible, not what's required next

patterns.title: Quick Feature
patterns.description: When you know what you want to build and just need to execute:
patterns.bestFor: Small to medium features, bug fixes, straightforward changes.

patterns.title: Exploratory
patterns.description: When requirements are unclear or you need to investigate first:
patterns.bestFor: Performance optimization, debugging, architectural decisions, unclear requirements.

patterns.title: Parallel Changes
patterns.description: Work on multiple changes at once:
patterns.bestFor: Parallel work streams, urgent interrupts, team collaboration.

ffVsContinue.situation: Exploring, want to review each step
Want to iterate on proposal before specs
Time pressure, need to move fast

updateVsNew: Learning-driven corrections (codebase isn't what you expected)
Design tweaks based on implementation discoveries
Scope exploded to different work entirely
Original change can be marked "done" standalone
Patches would confuse more than clarify
"Need to also support custom themes" → New change (scope exploded)
"System preference detection is harder than expected" → Update (same intent)
"Let's ship toggle first, add preferences later" → Update then archive, then new change
bestPractices: Keep Changes Focused：One logical unit of work per change. If you're doing "add feature X and also refactor Y", consider two separate changes.
Use /opsx:explore for Unclear Requirements：Before committing to a change, explore the problem space:
Verify Before Archiving：Use /opsx:verify to check implementation matches artifacts:
Name Changes Clearly：Good names make openspec list useful:

concepts.title: Philosophy
concepts.description: OpenSpec is built around four principles:

concepts.title: The Big Picture
concepts.description: OpenSpec organizes your work into two main areas:

concepts.title: Specs
concepts.description: Specs describe your system's behavior using structured requirements and scenarios.

concepts.title: Purpose
concepts.description: Authentication and session management for the application.

concepts.title: Requirements
concepts.description: The system SHALL issue a JWT token upon successful login.

concepts.title: Changes
concepts.description: A change is a proposed modification to your system, packaged as a folder with everything needed to understand and implement it.

concepts.title: Artifacts
concepts.description: Artifacts are the documents within a change that guide the work.

concepts.title: Intent
concepts.description: Users have requested a dark mode option to reduce eye strain during nighttime usage and match system preferences.

concepts.title: Approach
concepts.description: Use CSS custom properties for theming with a React context for state management. Detect system preference on first load, allow manual override.

concepts.title: Technical Approach
concepts.description: Theme state managed via React Context to avoid prop drilling. CSS custom properties enable runtime switching without class toggling.

concepts.title: Architecture Decisions
concepts.description: Using React Context for theme state because:

concepts.title: Delta Specs
concepts.description: Delta specs are the key concept that makes OpenSpec work for brownfield development. They describe what's changing rather than restating the entire spec.

concepts.title: ADDED Requirements
concepts.description: The system MUST support TOTP-based two-factor authentication.

concepts.title: MODIFIED Requirements
concepts.description: The system MUST expire sessions after 15 minutes of inactivity. (Previously: 30 minutes)

concepts.title: REMOVED Requirements
concepts.description: (Deprecated in favor of 2FA. Users should re-authenticate each session.)

concepts.title: Schemas
concepts.description: Schemas define the artifact types and their dependencies for a workflow.

concepts.title: Archive
concepts.description: Archiving completes a change by merging its delta specs into the main specs and preserving the change for history.

glossary.definition: A document within a change (proposal, design, tasks, or delta specs)
The process of completing a change and merging its deltas into main specs
A proposed modification to the system, packaged as a folder with artifacts
A spec that describes changes (ADDED/MODIFIED/REMOVED) relative to current specs
A logical grouping for specs (e.g., auth/, payments/)
A specific behavior the system must have
A concrete example of a requirement, typically in Given/When/Then format
A definition of artifact types and their dependencies
A specification describing system behavior, containing requirements and scenarios
The openspec/specs/ directory, containing the current agreed-upon behavior

gettingStarted.title: How It Works
gettingStarted.description: OpenSpec helps you and your AI coding assistant agree on what to build before any code is written.

gettingStarted.title: What OpenSpec Creates
gettingStarted.description: After running openspec init, your project has this structure:
bullets: specs/ - The source of truth. These specs describe how your system currently behaves. Organized by domain (e.g., specs/auth/, specs/payments/).
changes/ - Proposed modifications. Each change gets its own folder with all related artifacts. When a change is complete, its specs merge into the main specs/ directory.
codeBlocks:
openspec/
├── specs/              # Source of truth (your system's behavior)
│   └── <domain>/
│       └── spec.md
├── changes/            # Proposed updates (one folder per change)
│   └── <change-name>/
│       ├── proposal.md
│       ├── design.md
│       ├── tasks.md
│       └── specs/      # Delta specs (what's changing)
│           └── <domain>/
│               └── spec.md
└── config.yaml         # Project configuration (optional)

gettingStarted.title: Understanding Artifacts
gettingStarted.description: Each change folder contains artifacts that guide the work:
codeBlocks:
proposal ──► specs ──► design ──► tasks ──► implement
   ▲           ▲          ▲                    │
   └───────────┴──────────┴────────────────────┘
            update as you learn

gettingStarted.title: How Delta Specs Work
gettingStarted.description: Delta specs are the key concept in OpenSpec. They show what's changing relative to your current specs.

gettingStarted.title: 1. Start the Change (Default)
gettingStarted.description: If you've enabled the expanded workflow profile, you can also do this as two steps: /opsx:new then /opsx:ff (or /opsx:continue incrementally).
codeBlocks:
You: /opsx:propose add-dark-mode

AI:  Created openspec/changes/add-dark-mode/
     ✓ proposal.md — why we're doing this, what's changing
     ✓ specs/       — requirements and scenarios
     ✓ design.md    — technical approach
     ✓ tasks.md     — implementation checklist
     Ready for implementation!

gettingStarted.title: Verifying and Reviewing
gettingStarted.description: Use the CLI to check on your changes:
codeBlocks:
# List active changes
openspec list

# View change details
openspec show add-dark-mode

# Validate spec formatting
openspec validate add-dark-mode

# Interactive dashboard
openspec view

installation.title: Package Managers · npm
installation.description: 使用 npm 安装 OpenSpec。

installation.title: Package Managers · pnpm
installation.description: 使用 pnpm 安装 OpenSpec。

installation.title: Package Managers · yarn
installation.description: 使用 yarn 安装 OpenSpec。

installation.title: Package Managers · bun
installation.description: 使用 bun 安装 OpenSpec。

installation.title: Nix
Next Steps
installation.description: After installing, initialize OpenSpec in your project:

cli.name: openspec init
cli.description: Initialize OpenSpec in your project. Creates the folder structure and configures AI tool integrations.

args.name: path
args.description: Target directory (default: current directory)

options.name: --tools <list>
options.description: Configure AI tools non-interactively. Use all, none, or comma-separated list

options.name: --profile <profile>
options.description: Override global profile for this init run (core or custom)

cli.name: openspec update
cli.description: Update OpenSpec instruction files after upgrading the CLI. Re-generates AI tool configuration files using your current global profile, selected workflows, and delivery mode.

args.name: path
args.description: Target directory (default: current directory)

options.name: --force
options.description: Force update even when files are up to date

cli.name: openspec list
cli.description: List changes or specs in your project.

cli.name: openspec view
cli.description: Display an interactive dashboard for exploring specs and changes.

cli.name: openspec show
cli.description: Display details of a change or spec.

args.name: item-name
args.description: Name of change or spec (prompts if omitted)

options.name: --type <type>
options.description: Specify type: change or spec (auto-detected if unambiguous)

options.name: --requirements
options.description: Show only requirements, exclude scenarios (JSON mode)

options.name: -r, --requirement <id>
options.description: Show specific requirement by 1-based index (JSON mode)

cli.name: openspec validate
cli.description: Validate changes and specs for structural issues.

args.name: item-name
args.description: Specific item to validate (prompts if omitted)

options.name: --type <type>
options.description: Specify type when name is ambiguous: change or spec

options.name: --concurrency <n>
options.description: Max parallel validations (default: 6, or OPENSPEC_CONCURRENCY env)

cli.name: openspec archive
cli.description: Archive a completed change and merge delta specs into main specs.

args.name: change-name
args.description: Change to archive (prompts if omitted)

cli.name: openspec status
cli.description: Display artifact completion status for a change.

cli.name: openspec instructions
cli.description: Get enriched instructions for creating an artifact or applying tasks. Used by AI agents to understand what to create next.

args.name: artifact
args.description: Artifact ID: proposal, specs, design, tasks, or apply

cli.name: openspec templates
cli.description: Show resolved template paths for all artifacts in a schema.

cli.name: openspec schemas
cli.description: List available workflow schemas with their descriptions and artifact flows.

args.name: name
args.description: Schema name (kebab-case)

options.name: --no-default
options.description: Don't prompt to set as default

cli.name: openspec schema fork
cli.description: Copy an existing schema to your project for customization.

args.name: source
args.description: Schema to copy

args.name: name
args.description: New schema name (default: <source>-custom)

cli.name: openspec schema validate
cli.description: Validate a schema's structure and templates.

args.name: name
args.description: Schema to validate (validates all if omitted)

cli.name: openspec schema which
cli.description: Show where a schema resolves from (useful for debugging precedence).

args.name: name
args.description: Schema name

options.name: --all
options.description: List all schemas with their sources

cli.name: openspec config
cli.description: View and modify global OpenSpec configuration.

cli.name: openspec feedback
cli.description: Submit feedback about OpenSpec. Creates a GitHub issue.

args.name: message
args.description: Feedback message

cli.name: openspec completion
cli.description: Manage shell completions for the OpenSpec CLI.

customization.title: Project Configuration
customization.description: The openspec/config.yaml file is the easiest way to customize OpenSpec for your team. It lets you:
examples:
# openspec/config.yaml
schema: spec-driven

context: |
  Tech stack: TypeScript, React, Node.js, PostgreSQL
  API style: RESTful, documented in docs/api.md
  Testing: Jest + React Testing Library
  We value backwards compatibility for all public APIs

rules:
  proposal:
    - Include rollback plan
    - Identify affected teams
  specs:
    - Use Given/When/Then format
    - Reference existing patterns before inventing new ones

# Without config
openspec new change my-feature --schema spec-driven

# With config - schema is automatic
openspec new change my-feature

<context>
Tech stack: TypeScript, React, Node.js, PostgreSQL
...
</context>

<rules>
- Include rollback plan
- Identify affected teams
</rules>

<template>
[Schema's built-in template]
</template>

customization.title: Custom Schemas
customization.description: When project config isn't enough, create your own schema with a completely custom workflow. Custom schemas live in your project's openspec/schemas/ directory and are version-controlled with your code.

options.name: id
options.purpose: Unique identifier, used in commands and rules

options.name: instruction
options.purpose: AI instructions for creating this artifact

options.name: requires
options.purpose: Dependencies - which artifacts must exist first

examples:
your-project/
├── openspec/
│   ├── config.yaml        # Project config
│   ├── schemas/           # Custom schemas live here
│   │   └── my-workflow/
│   │       ├── schema.yaml
│   │       └── templates/
│   └── changes/           # Your changes
└── src/

openspec/schemas/my-workflow/
├── schema.yaml           # Workflow definition
└── templates/
    ├── proposal.md       # Template for proposal artifact
    ├── spec.md           # Template for specs
    ├── design.md         # Template for design
    └── tasks.md          # Template for tasks

# Interactive
openspec schema init research-first

# Non-interactive
openspec schema init rapid \
  --description "Rapid iteration workflow" \
  --artifacts "proposal,tasks" \
  --default

# openspec/schemas/my-workflow/schema.yaml
name: my-workflow
version: 1
description: My team's custom workflow

artifacts:
  - id: proposal
    generates: proposal.md
    description: Initial proposal document
    template: proposal.md
    instruction: |
      Create a proposal that explains WHY this change is needed.
      Focus on the problem, not the solution.
    requires: []

  - id: design
    generates: design.md
    description: Technical design
    template: design.md
    instruction: |
      Create a design document explaining HOW to implement.
    requires:
      - proposal    # Can't create design until proposal exists

  - id: tasks
    generates: tasks.md
    description: Implementation checklist
    template: tasks.md
    requires:
      - design

apply:
  requires: [tasks]
  tracks: tasks.md

customization.title: Why
customization.description: <!-- Explain the motivation for this change. What problem does this solve? -->

customization.title: What Changes
customization.description: <!-- Describe what will change. Be specific about new capabilities or modifications. -->

customization.title: Impact
customization.description: <!-- Affected code, APIs, dependencies, systems --> openspec schema validate my-workflow
examples:
Templates can include:
- Section headers the AI should fill in
- HTML comments with guidance for the AI
- Example formats showing expected structure

### Validate Your Schema

Before using a custom schema, validate it:

This checks:
- `schema.yaml` syntax is correct
- All referenced templates exist
- No circular dependencies
- Artifact IDs are valid

### Use Your Custom Schema

Once created, use your schema with:

### Debug Schema Resolution

Not sure which schema is being used? Check with:
Output shows whether it's from your project, user directory, or the package:

customization.title: Examples
customization.description: A minimal workflow for quick iterations:
examples:
# openspec/schemas/rapid/schema.yaml
name: rapid
version: 1
description: Fast iteration with minimal overhead

artifacts:
  - id: proposal
    generates: proposal.md
    description: Quick proposal
    template: proposal.md
    instruction: |
      Create a brief proposal for this change.
      Focus on what and why, skip detailed specs.
    requires: []

  - id: tasks
    generates: tasks.md
    description: Implementation checklist
    template: tasks.md
    requires: [proposal]

apply:
  requires: [tasks]
  tracks: tasks.md

- id: review
    generates: review.md
    description: Pre-implementation review checklist
    template: review.md
    instruction: |
      Create a review checklist based on the design.
      Include security, performance, and testing considerations.
    requires:
      - design

  - id: tasks
    # ... existing tasks config ...
    requires:
      - specs
      - design
      - review    # Now tasks require review too

migrationGuide.title: What's Changing?
migrationGuide.description: OPSX replaces the old phase-locked workflow with a fluid, action-based approach. Here's the key shift:

migrationGuide.title: Before You Begin
migrationGuide.description: The migration process is designed with preservation in mind:
bullets: Active changes in openspec/changes/ — Completely preserved. You can continue them with OPSX commands.
Archived changes — Untouched. Your history remains intact.
Main specs in openspec/specs/ — Untouched. These are your source of truth.
Your content in CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, etc. — Preserved. Only the OpenSpec marker blocks are removed; everything you wrote stays.
Claude Code: .claude/commands/openspec/
Cursor: .cursor/commands/openspec-*.md
Windsurf: .windsurf/workflows/openspec-*.md
Cline: .clinerules/workflows/openspec-*.md
Roo: .roo/commands/openspec-*.md
GitHub Copilot: .github/prompts/openspec-*.prompt.md (IDE extensions only; not supported in Copilot CLI)
And others (Augment, Continue, Amazon Q, etc.)
Tech stack and key conventions
Non-obvious constraints the AI needs to know
Rules that frequently got ignored before

migrationGuide.title: Running the Migration
migrationGuide.description: Both openspec init and openspec update detect legacy files and guide you through the same cleanup process. Use whichever fits your situation:
bullets: New installs default to profile core (propose, explore, apply, archive).
Migrated installs preserve your previously installed workflows by writing a custom profile when needed.

migrationGuide.title: Migrating project.md to config.yaml
migrationGuide.description: The old openspec/project.md was a freeform markdown file for project context. The new openspec/config.yaml is structured and—critically—injected into every planning request so your conventions are always present when the AI works.

migrationGuide.title: Conventions
bullets: All public APIs must maintain backwards compatibility
New features should include tests
Use Given/When/Then format for specifications
Include rollback plan for risky changes
Use Given/When/Then format for scenarios
Reference existing patterns before inventing new ones
Include sequence diagrams for complex flows
Tech stack (languages, frameworks, databases)
Key architectural patterns (monorepo, microservices, etc.)
Non-obvious constraints ("we can't use library X because...")
Critical conventions that often get ignored
Artifact-specific formatting ("use Given/When/Then in specs")
Review criteria ("proposals must include rollback plans")
These only appear for the matching artifact, keeping other requests lighter
General best practices the AI already knows
Verbose explanations that could be summarized
Historical context that doesn't affect current work
Your proposal-specific guidance
Your spec-writing rules

migrationGuide.title: Understanding the New Architecture
migrationGuide.description: The legacy workflow forced linear progression:

migrationGuide.title: Continuing Existing Changes
migrationGuide.description: Your in-progress changes work seamlessly with OPSX commands.

migrationGuide.title: The New Config System
migrationGuide.description: When determining which schema to use, OPSX checks in order:
bullets: Include rollback plan
Use Given/When/Then format
Document fallback strategies
Break into 2-hour maximum chunks

migrationGuide.title: Troubleshooting
migrationGuide.description: You're running in a CI or non-interactive environment. Use:
bullets: spec-driven: proposal, specs, design, tasks

migrationGuide.title: Quick Reference
bullets: .claude/commands/openspec/ — replaced by .claude/skills/
openspec/AGENTS.md — obsolete
openspec/project.md — migrate to config.yaml, then delete
OpenSpec marker blocks in CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, etc.

sections.title: What Is It?
sections.description: OPSX is now the standard workflow for OpenSpec.

sections.title: Why This Exists
sections.description: The legacy OpenSpec workflow works, but it's locked down:
bullets: Instructions are hardcoded — buried in TypeScript, you can't change them
All-or-nothing — one big command creates everything, can't test individual pieces
Fixed structure — same workflow for everyone, no customization
Black box — when AI output is bad, you can't tweak the prompts
Teams — create workflows that match how you actually work
Power users — tweak prompts to get better AI outputs for your codebase
OpenSpec contributors — experiment with new approaches without releases

sections.title: The User Experience
sections.description: The problem with linear workflows: You're "in planning phase", then "in implementation phase", then "done". But real work doesn't work that way. You implement something, realize your design was wrong, need to update specs, continue implementing. Linear phases fight against how work actually happens.
bullets: Actions, not phases — create, implement, update, archive — do any of them anytime
Dependencies are enablers — they show what's possible, not what's required next

sections.title: Setup
sections.description: This creates skills in .claude/skills/ (or equivalent) that AI coding assistants auto-detect.

sections.title: Project Configuration
sections.description: Project config lets you set defaults and inject project-specific context into all artifacts.
bullets: Include rollback plan
Identify affected teams
Use Given/When/Then format for scenarios
Include sequence diagrams for complex flows
Context is prepended to every artifact's instructions
Wrapped in <context>...</context> tags
Helps AI understand your project's conventions
Rules are only injected for matching artifacts
Wrapped in <rules>...</rules> tags
Appear after context, before the template
proposal — Change proposal
specs — Specifications
design — Technical design
tasks — Implementation tasks
Unknown artifact IDs in rules generate warnings
Schema names are validated against available schemas
Context has a 50KB size limit
Invalid YAML is reported with line numbers
Check artifact IDs match your schema (see list above)
Run openspec schemas --json to see artifact IDs for each schema
Ensure file is at openspec/config.yaml (not .yml)
Check YAML syntax with a validator
Config changes take effect immediately (no restart needed)
Context is limited to 50KB
Summarize or link to external docs instead

sections.title: Usage
sections.description: Think through ideas, investigate problems, compare options. No structure required - just a thinking partner. When insights crystallize, transition to /opsx:propose (default) or /opsx:new//opsx:ff (expanded).

sections.title: When to Update vs. Start Fresh
sections.description: You can always edit your proposal or specs before implementation. But when does refining become "this is different work"?
bullets: You discover edge cases you didn't consider
The approach needs tweaking but the goal is unchanged
Implementation reveals the design was slightly off
You realize full scope is too big, want to ship MVP first
"Add dark mode" → "Add dark mode toggle (system preference in v2)"
Codebase isn't structured how you thought
A dependency doesn't work as expected
"Use CSS variables" → "Use Tailwind's dark: prefix instead"
The problem itself is different now
"Add dark mode" → "Add comprehensive theme system with custom colors, fonts, spacing"
Change grew so much it's essentially different work
Original proposal would be unrecognizable after updates
"Fix login bug" → "Rewrite auth system"
The original change can be marked "done"
New work stands alone, not a refinement
Complete "Add dark mode MVP" → Archive → New change "Enhance dark mode"
Keep committing while working on the same feature
Start a new branch when it's genuinely new work
Sometimes merge a partial feature and start fresh for phase 2

sections.title: What's Different?
sections.description: The key insight: work isn't linear. OPSX stops pretending it is.

sections.title: Architecture Deep Dive
sections.description: This section explains how OPSX works under the hood and how it compares to the legacy workflow. Examples in this section use the expanded command set (new, continue, etc.); default core users can map the same flow to propose → apply → archive.
bullets: id: research # Added before proposal
id: proposal
id: tasks

sections.title: Schemas
sections.description: Schemas define what artifacts exist and their dependencies. Currently available:
bullets: spec-driven (default): proposal → specs → design → tasks

sections.title: Tips
bullets: Use /opsx:explore to think through an idea before committing to a change
/opsx:ff when you know what you want, /opsx:continue when exploring
During /opsx:apply, if something's wrong — fix the artifact, then continue
Tasks track progress via checkboxes in tasks.md
Check status anytime: openspec status --change "name"

sections.title: Feedback
sections.description: This is rough. That's intentional — we're learning what works.

commands.description: Create a change and generate planning artifacts in one step (default quick path)
Think through ideas, investigate problems, clarify requirements
Start a new change scaffold (expanded workflow)
Create the next artifact (expanded workflow)
Fast-forward planning artifacts (expanded workflow)
Implement tasks, updating artifacts as needed
Validate implementation against artifacts (expanded workflow)
Sync delta specs to main (expanded workflow, optional)
Archive when done
Archive multiple completed changes (expanded workflow)
Guided walkthrough of an end-to-end change (expanded workflow)
